__meta__.description: The Fluttering Sail — Philosophical Stopword List
__meta__.note: Words in this list are excluded from ingestion by default. They appear pre-unchecked in the Coverage Report review step. A researcher may override any entry via the UI checkbox. Edit this file to add domain-specific words that are trivial in your context, or REMOVE words that carry philosophical weight in your domain. One researcher's stopword is another's keyword — this list is intentionally conservative.
__meta__.source: Derived from NLTK English stopwords corpus plus common verbs and generic nouns with no context-independent philosophical specificity.
__meta__.methodology_note: Borderline words (e.g. 'word', 'air', 'water', 'lord') are deliberately NOT in this list. Their philosophical weight is context-dependent and should be decided by the researcher at review time.

_note_how_to_edit: Add a word to exclude it from ingestion by default. Remove a word to make it appear in the philosophical vocabulary column (pre-checked) in the review UI. All changes take effect immediately without restarting the app.

articles_determiners._note: Function words with no semantic content independent of context.

prepositions._note: Spatial and relational prepositions with no standalone philosophical weight.

auxiliaries._note: Auxiliary and modal verbs. Note: 'be', 'will', 'should' have philosophical uses in normative contexts but are too ambiguous at the token level to be reliable signals.

adverbs_particles._note: Common adverbs and particles. 'Never', 'always', 'only' can carry normative weight in context but are too ambiguous as standalone tokens.

generic_nouns._note: High-frequency nouns whose philosophical import depends entirely on context. Researchers in phenomenology or philosophy of time may want to remove 'time', 'place', 'form' from this list.

generic_verbs._note: High-frequency verbs with no reliable philosophical signal at the token level. 'Know', 'think', 'feel' have epistemological uses but are too common in non-philosophical contexts to be reliable.

contractions._note: Diacritic-stripped contraction forms that appear after tokenisation.

numbers._note: Cardinal and ordinal numbers as words.
